QuestionText: According to rowing safety best practices, when is high-visibility clothing recommended for rowers?
AnswerA: Only during organized regattas
AnswerB: Always while on the water, especially during low light conditions
AnswerC: Only when specifically instructed by a coach
Explanation: High-visibility clothing is strongly recommended at all times while on the water, and is particularly important during low light conditions including dawn, dusk, cloudy days, and fog to ensure maximum visibility to other water users.

QuestionText: What is the recommended high-visibility clothing for rowing?
AnswerA: Any bright-colored shirt
AnswerB: A high-visibility vest or outer layer in yellow, orange, or bright green
AnswerC: A flashing light attached to the boat
Explanation: A high-visibility vest or outer layer in yellow, orange, or bright green is recommended for all rowing activities to enhance visibility to other water users.

QuestionText: Which boat lights are required by Michigan boating laws for rowing during dawn, dusk, or in low light conditions?
AnswerA: A white light on the bow only
AnswerB: A white light on the stern only
AnswerC: A white light visible in time to avoid a collision
AnswerD: Any flashing light visible from all directions
Explanation: Michigan boating laws require a white light visible in time to avoid a collision when rowing before sunrise or after sunset. For scullers on the Huron River, clip-on lights at bow and stern are commonly used.

QuestionText: Who is eligible to row at AARC?
AnswerA: Anyone who has passed a swim test
AnswerB: Club members and class participants who have paid all fees and signed waivers
AnswerC: Anyone who knows how to row
AnswerD: People who have their own equipment
Explanation: Only club members and class participants who have paid all fees may row, and each must have signed the AARC liability membership waiver and the US Rowing Basic Waiver and Release of Liability Agreement.

QuestionText: Where should life jackets be kept while rowing?
AnswerA: Life jackets are not required for rowing
AnswerB: Either in the coach's launch or in the boat
AnswerC: Always on your person while rowing
Explanation: Every rower must have a life jacket accessible, either in the coach's launch or in the boat when rowing without a coach.

QuestionText: What should you do if sudden severe weather strikes while on the water?
AnswerA: Continue rowing but be more careful
AnswerB: Vacate the water immediately, signal all crews, and account for everyone
AnswerD: Row faster to outrace the weather
Explanation: If sudden severe weather strikes (lightning, thunder, high wind, etc.), vacate the water immediately, signal all crews (e.g., air horn), and account for everyone before departing.

QuestionText: If you're caught on the Huron River as it's getting dark and you don't have lights, what should you do?
AnswerA: Continue rowing but more carefully
AnswerB: Return to the dock while strictly following traffic patterns and yielding to all other vessels
AnswerC: Turn on your mobile phone flashlight as a substitute
AnswerD: Increase your stroke rate to get back faster
Explanation: If you don't have proper lighting and visibility is decreasing, return to the dock while strictly following traffic patterns. Take extra caution, row slowly, stay close to the shoreline when appropriate, and yield to all other vessels that may not see you.

QuestionText: How does wearing high-visibility clothing complement boat lighting for safety?
AnswerA: It makes you visible if you fall into the water away from your boat
AnswerB: It matches the club colors
AnswerC: It keeps you warmer in cold conditions
AnswerD: It helps identify your skill level to others
Explanation: High-visibility clothing ensures you remain visible if separated from your boat, such as during a capsize, when boat lights would no longer help identify your position.

QuestionText: When are unaccompanied single or double sculls allowed on the water?
AnswerA: Any time during daylight hours
AnswerB: Only when specifically approved by the Director after water/air temperatures are deemed safe
AnswerD: Any time, as long as you've taken a safety course
Explanation: Unaccompanied use of single (1x) or double (2-) sculls is prohibited until water and air temperatures are deemed safe; the Director will notify when conditions allow.

QuestionText: What must be done before taking a shell on the water?
AnswerA: Nothing, just take the boat out
AnswerB: Sign out the shell in the boathouse logbook, noting boat name and departure time
AnswerC: Get verbal permission from a coach
Explanation: All shells must be signed out in the boathouse logbook (noting boat name and departure time) before going on the water, and any equipment issues reported upon return.

QuestionText: True or False: If you're wearing high-visibility clothing, you don't need to use boat lights at dawn or dusk.
AnswerA: TRUE
AnswerB: FALSE
Explanation: False. High-visibility clothing and boat lights serve complementary safety purposes. Michigan law requires lights regardless of clothing during dawn, dusk, and low light conditions for all vessels on the Huron River.

QuestionText: Which color combination for high-visibility clothing is most effective for being seen on the water?
AnswerA: Black with reflective strips
AnswerB: Dark blue with yellow accents
Explanation: Fluorescent yellow, orange, or green provide the highest visibility on water, especially in low light conditions, making these the most effective colors for safety.

QuestionText: What should you do if you notice an equipment issue with a shell after rowing?
AnswerA: Fix it yourself if you can
AnswerB: Don't worry about it unless it affected your rowing
AnswerC: Report it in the logbook upon return
Explanation: Any equipment issues must be reported in the logbook upon returning the shell to the boathouse.

QuestionText: During group rows in low light conditions, who is responsible for ensuring all boats have proper lighting and high-visibility clothing?
AnswerA: Only the coach
AnswerB: Only the club safety officer
AnswerD: The most experienced rower in each boat
Explanation: While coaches and safety officers provide guidance, each individual rower is ultimately responsible for ensuring they have proper safety equipment, including lights and high-visibility clothing.

QuestionText: How does proper lighting affect your legal status on shared waterways like the Huron River?
AnswerA: It's optional but recommended
AnswerB: It's required by Michigan boating laws for all vessels
AnswerC: It's only required for racing shells
AnswerD: It's only required for motorized vessels
Explanation: Proper lighting is required by Michigan boating laws for all vessels, including rowing shells, during low light conditions, and failure to comply can result in legal penalties as well as safety risks.

QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 30/30 rule regarding lightning safety?
AnswerA: Row at 30 strokes per minute for 30 minutes during a storm
AnswerB: If the time between lightning and thunder is less than 30 seconds, or lightning is seen within 30 minutes of the end of a storm, remain off the water
AnswerC: Keep at least 30 meters from other boats during lightning
AnswerD: Wait 30 minutes after eating before rowing to avoid lightning strikes
Explanation: The 30/30 rule states that if the time between seeing lightning and hearing thunder is less than 30 seconds (indicating the storm is within 6 miles), or if lightning is seen within 30 minutes of the end of a storm, remain off the water.

QuestionText: What is the overarching safety principle at AARC?
AnswerA: Follow rules only when coaches are watching
AnswerB: Always prioritize speed and technique over safety
AnswerC: Use common sense and avoid any behavior that could endanger people or equipment
AnswerD: Only advanced rowers need to worry about safety
Explanation: Always prioritize safety—avoid any behavior that could endanger people or equipment.

QuestionText: What two official waivers must be signed before participating in AARC rowing activities?
AnswerA: A medical waiver and equipment damage waiver
AnswerB: The AARC liability membership waiver and the US Rowing Basic Waiver
AnswerC: A swimming test confirmation and weather acknowledgment
AnswerD: A personal boat usage agreement and insurance waiver
Explanation: All rowers must have signed the AARC liability membership waiver and the US Rowing Basic Waiver and Release of Liability Agreement before participating.

QuestionText: True or False: It's acceptable for Learn-to-Scull participants to practice in a single shell without a safety launch if they stay close to shore.
AnswerA: TRUE
AnswerB: FALSE
Explanation: False. No Learn-to-Scull participant may practice from a single shell unless there is also a safety launch in attendance, regardless of proximity to shore.

QuestionText: What should single scullers carry with them during workouts?
AnswerA: A bottle of water
AnswerB: A cell phone or whistle
Explanation: Single scullers must carry a cell phone or whistle with them during workouts.
